title: Reading and Use of English – Part 5
description: You are going to read a magazine article about an intellectual process known as critical thinking. For questions 31 – 36, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which you think fits best according to the text. Mark your answers on the separate answer sheet.

content.title: The Science of Sleep: The Brain’s Nightly Maintenance
content.subtitle: Why resting is not a passive state, but a critical period of neural cleaning.
content.text: For decades, sleep was viewed by science as a period of 'dormancy'—a simple shutting down of the body to conserve energy. However, recent breakthroughs in neuroscience have revealed that the sleeping brain is arguably as active as the waking one, albeit in a very different way. [31] The writer suggests that sleep is less like a 'power down' and more like a 'night shift' at a factory, where essential maintenance and repairs are carried out that cannot happen during the noise of the day. [/31] Central to this is the 'glymphatic system,' a recently discovered waste-clearance pathway that flushes out toxic byproducts that accumulate between neurons while we think and act. || This cellular cleaning is particularly important for the prevention of neurodegenerative diseases. During deep sleep, the space between brain cells increases by up to 60%, allowing cerebrospinal fluid to wash through the brain like a biological dishwasher. [32] Without this nightly rinse, the buildup of proteins like beta-amyloid can lead to cognitive decline, proving that sleep is not a luxury, but a fundamental requirement for long-term brain health. [/32] We are starting to understand that many of the cognitive issues associated with aging may actually be the result of a lifetime of poor 'sleep hygiene.' || [33] The author's tone when discussing the 'sleep-deprived professional' is one of quiet irony. [/33] In many modern corporate cultures, surviving on four hours of sleep is seen as a 'badge of honor'—a sign of dedication and grit. Yet, research shows that a brain deprived of sleep is functionally equivalent to one that is legally intoxicated. Our ability to solve complex problems, regulate our emotions, and even read social cues is severely compromised. By prioritizing work over rest, these professionals are inadvertently sabotaging the very productivity they claim to value. They are running on fumes while convinced they are at peak performance. || Beyond physical cleaning, sleep is also the period when the brain performs 'memory consolidation.' [34] During the REM (Rapid Eye Movement) phase, the brain sifts through the day's experiences, deciding what to keep and what to discard, effectively moving information from short-term to long-term storage. [/34] This is why students who 'pull an all-nighter' often perform worse on exams than those who sleep; they may have read the information, but they haven't given their brain the time to 'save the file.' Sleep is where learning actually becomes knowledge. || [35] However, some experts argue that the rise of artificial light and blue-screen technology is causing a 'circadian crisis.' [/35] Our bodies are biologically programmed to respond to the cycle of the sun, but our modern environment is one of 'perpetual noon.' This suppresses the production of melatonin, the hormone that signals to the body that it is time to rest. We are living in a state of biological jet lag, disconnected from the natural rhythms that have guided human evolution for millennia. The consequences are only now beginning to manifest in rising rates of metabolic and mental health disorders. || Ultimately, we must reconsider our relationship with the pillow. [36] The writer concludes that the most productive thing we can do for our future selves is to respect our need for downtime today. [/36] It is an invitation to move away from a 'hustle' culture that views rest as a weakness and toward a 'wellness' culture that views it as a strategic advantage. In the race for success, the person who sleeps well may actually be the one who finishes first. The brain is a masterpiece of biological engineering, but even the best machines need time for maintenance.

questions.question: In the first paragraph, the author uses the 'night shift' analogy to imply that...
options: A) the brain stops working entirely during the night to save energy.
B) sleep is a highly productive period for specific biological processes.
C) most factory workers prefer to work at night rather than during the day.
D) the brain is more easily distracted by noise during the evening.
questions.explanation: B is supported by [31] and the idea of 'essential maintenance' that cannot happen during the day. A is the 'dormancy' view the author rejects. C and D are literal misinterpretations.

questions.question: According to the second paragraph, the 'glymphatic system' helps prevent disease by...
options: A) increasing the speed at which neurons communicate with each other.
B) removing harmful proteins that build up in the brain during the day.
C) allowing humans to survive without sleep for longer periods of time.
D) replacing the need for physical exercise in maintaining brain health.
questions.explanation: B is supported by [32] and the mention of flushing out 'toxic byproducts' and proteins like beta-amyloid. A, C, and D are not mentioned or are contradicted by the text.

questions.question: What is the author's view of professionals who brag about their lack of sleep?
options: A) He admires their dedication and their ability to work under pressure.
B) He believes they are more productive than those who sleep eight hours.
C) He finds it ironic because their lack of sleep actually ruins their work.
D) He thinks they are the only people who can succeed in the modern economy.
questions.explanation: C is supported by [33] and the mention of 'quiet irony' because they are 'sabotaging the very productivity they claim to value.' A, B, and D are the opposite of the author's critical view.

questions.question: The 'memory consolidation' mentioned in the fourth paragraph explains why...
options: A) reading before bed is the best way to fall asleep quickly.
B) people who do not sleep well often forget the information they learned.
C) students should stay up all night to memorize as much as possible.
D) short-term memory is more important for exam success than long-term memory.
questions.explanation: B is supported by [34] and the comparison of sleep to 'saving the file.' C is explicitly advised against. A and D are not mentioned.

questions.question: What does the author mean by a 'circadian crisis' in the fifth paragraph?
options: A) A sudden shortage of melatonin in the global pharmaceutical market.
B) A state where people are forced to work only during the night.
C) A disruption of the body's internal clock caused by artificial light.
D) The inability of the human body to adapt to seasonal weather changes.
questions.explanation: C is supported by [35] and the mention of 'perpetual noon' suppressing melatonin. A, B, and D are not the meaning of the term in this context.

questions.question: In the final paragraph, the writer suggests that we should view sleep as...
options: A) a sign of physical and mental weakness in a competitive world.
B) a luxury that should only be enjoyed on the weekends.
C) a strategic tool that actually improves our chances of success.
D) something that will become less important as technology improves.
questions.explanation: C is supported by [36] and the call to view rest as a 'strategic advantage.' A is the 'hustle' culture view the author wants to leave behind. B and D are not suggested.
